But my dad said that was the best year for him here because everybody had all kinds, and he could raise his cattle and get cheap feed. Because they couldn't because they wanted to trade it for wood or trade it for anything you had, or a wood or post or whatever he had. So he always said that was a good year for him. In fact, I think lumber was cheap them days. We had a big house here that burnt down where all of us grew up, you know. And the log house, and then the big house, and then this little house.

And my mother always said she thought it was heaven when she got here. They didn't have so many different kinds of bugs and a few things, but although I've heard her say lots of times, if they'd had money enough to get away, they wouldn't have stayed. They didn't know where they'd go, but they'd have to stay because they didn't have any money. Of course, that was the way everybody was them days, you know. You didn't have so much money. You know, Mita didn't, wasn't a Harrison girl. Mrs. Harrison took her when her mother died. You didn't know that. Well, she did. She just thought somebody had to take her mother died. And they had to go someplace in the car every day, you know, like we do nowadays. But they did visit. And in the winter, after I was married and even before, people, we used to have parties around the houses and dances and play cards and just have a general good time. Didn't have to run to town like we used to now. So I don't know. That's the problem with having cars. That's right. And I don't know.

And then when our dad was dead, she'd come home here to help take care of him, her husband had passed away then, so then she just stayed, so I haven't lived with her on the line, we've been together the last 20 years, but there's probably 20 or 30, pretty 30 years in the community that we wasn't together. She went to France during World War I, and she took part of it, and then got married, and then her and her husband separated for a while, and she went back and finished, and went to France, and when we come back, we met her, and we lived happily ever after, but she was a faculty member, so then she came back, she graduated from St. Vincent in Portland, and we went back, we went back there, because our head and other sisters who lived in Portland, but it had changed so, since she didn't, couldn't you know how things feel, she didn't couldn't feel the line, they rode up, so I mean built up, so around it, because it just didn't look familiar at all.

That's how big the creek used to be, and in the spring it would get so big, we had a raft and floated around there, and so forth, and now you wouldn't believe it what these little tricks are. So, things have really changed. And her family used to get so big, and for my, you know, Bud Hill sister drowned in former down there, and they lived for the Macaulay's here, you know, you know, the Two Hill places and all that, but folks owned for the Macaulay's here, and she drowned someplace along there, and they found a body way down there, the Four Mile Schoolhouse, you know, or the old hospital sixes, maybe not even, maybe five, two little girls playing, and one may kind of cross the stream on a board or something, I think that's the way out. You know where the Four Mile Schoolhouse used to be, or do you?

speaker: Unknown Interviewer
words: No, I don't really.

speaker: Fannie Cuthbert Byers
words: Well, the Parker, where Graham used to be in Franklin. Oh, right there where that house...
